avoid: Increasing block size further without shape dispatch, as it may hurt occupancy for some shapes.
Attempting a custom full GEMM for W@X, since cuBLAS is already highly optimized and the bottleneck is the rank-16 update.
Hardcoding hidden dimensions or assuming fixed d values.
evidence: Aggregate speedup is only 1.008x, essentially no improvement over baseline.
At d=4096, speedup is 0.9925 (slight regression), while d=3584 shows marginal gain (1.0386) and d=4608 is neutral (0.9995).
Correctness is perfect (max_abs_err=0.0, rel_l2_err=0.0) so no numerical issues.
Profile summary indicates bottleneck is the rank-16 update or memory bandwidth, not the cuBLAS W@X.
Candidate increased block size from parent (likely 256) to 512, aiming to improve occupancy for larger d, but this did not yield meaningful speedup and caused a small regression at 4096.

recommended_actions: try float4 stores when d is divisible by 4
fall back to ATen if numerical error appears
Experiment with a range of block sizes (128, 256, 512, 1024) and implement shape-aware dispatch to select the best block size per d.
Profile memory bandwidth utilization (e.g., with ncu) to confirm if the kernel is memory-bound; if so, focus on improving memory coalescing and reducing redundant loads.
Consider using shared memory to cache the intermediate (B.T @ X) if not already done, to reduce global memory traffic.
Evaluate unrolling the inner loops further or using vectorized loads/stores (already vec4) to maximize bandwidth usage.
Investigate launch overhead: for smaller d, kernel launch might be significant; consider merging the rank-16 update into a single kernel or using CUDA graphs.
